Name: rate_distortion_pruning
Title: Information-Theoretic Neural Network Pruning via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: Neural network pruning can be formulated as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the distortion of the model's output while reducing the number of parameters, and this formulation can lead to more efficient and effective pruning strategies.
Related Work: Recent works such as [1] and [2] have explored the use of rate-distortion theory for neural network compression and pruning. These works have shown that this approach can lead to significant improvements in compression ratios and accuracy. Our proposal builds upon these works by exploring the use of rate-distortion theory for pruning, with a focus on developing novel methodologies and approaches that are guided by fundamental theoretical principles.
Abstract: This proposal explores the application of rate-distortion theory to neural network pruning, with the goal of developing more efficient and effective pruning strategies. We will investigate the use of rate-distortion theory to derive novel pruning algorithms that minimize the distortion of the model's output while reducing the number of parameters. Our approach will be grounded in the principles of information theory and statistical learning, and will involve the development of novel methodologies and approaches that are guided by fundamental theoretical principles.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to evaluate the performance of our proposed pruning algorithms on a range of benchmark datasets and models. Our evaluation metrics will include compression ratio, accuracy, and distortion. We will also compare our approach to existing pruning algorithms and evaluate the trade-offs between compression ratio, accuracy, and distortion.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that the development of novel pruning algorithms may require significant computational resources and expertise. Additionally, the evaluation of our approach may be limited by the availability of suitable benchmark datasets and models. To mitigate these risks, we will leverage existing computational resources and collaborate with experts in the field to ensure the development and evaluation of our approach.
